themes.name: Custom AI Silicon & Chip Architecture Licensing
themes.description: The move is being driven mainly by renewed enthusiasm around AI and data center spending: markets are rotating back into high‑bandwidth connectivity, custom silicon/IP, and semiconductor names leveraged to AI infrastructure. CRDO, AMD, INTC, QUIK, CEVA and ALAB all sit in that ecosystem (optical/interconnect, CPUs/GPUs, accelerators, FPGA/ASIC and IP), so they are rising on the sector-wide AI/semis momentum rather than a single company-specific headline. In addition, expectations for continued strong cloud capex and easing macro/rate concerns are supporting the broader semiconductor complex.

themes.name: AI Memory & Storage
themes.description: The main driver is renewed buying in the broader AI and data-center “full-stack” trade, where investors are rotating into memory, storage, and controller names as key infrastructure beneficiaries of accelerating AI workloads, not just GPUs. A widely-circulated “AI stack” callout this week explicitly highlighted memory and storage as the next leg of the AI trade, grouping MU, WDC, STX, SNDK and related names as critical suppliers to HBM, SSDs, and enterprise storage demand. That thematic tailwind, alongside expectations for stronger cloud/AI capex in upcoming quarters, is pulling the whole complex (including niche MRAM and controller vendor SIMO) higher in sympathy.

themes.name: Crypto Asset Recovery
themes.description: The move in RIOT and HUT this week is most likely being driven by a broader rebound in Bitcoin and renewed speculation around the crypto miners’ operating leverage, rather than a company-specific headline. Without a fresh catalyst in hand, the strength usually tracks Bitcoin price action, rising trading volume, and expectations that lower energy costs or improving hashprice will boost margins. If you want, I can help you pinpoint the exact headline or event behind the move once you share the day’s price action or a news snippet.

themes.name: Semiconductor Wafer Foundry & Advanced IC Manufacturing
themes.description: The move is being driven by a broader rotation back into semicap and specialty semiconductor names on expectations of sustained wafer fab equipment spending tied to AI, advanced packaging, and leading-edge node capacity builds. In particular, commentary from major chipmakers and foundries about increasing capex for AI/data center and 3 nm/2 nm ramps is reinforcing the view that the 2025–26 WFE cycle will be stronger and longer than previously modeled, lifting equipment, test, and materials suppliers together. There does not appear to be a single stock-specific headline for these names this week; they are trading as a group on the improved sector outlook and short-covering in previously lagging fabs, testers, and sub-suppliers.

themes.name: Power Semiconductor & Mixed-Signal Display Drivers
themes.description: HIMX, NVTS, and AOSL are all being lifted by the same broad AI/data-center power and semiconductor momentum, with NVTS getting a specific boost from its NVIDIA MGX collaboration on 800V AI rack power infrastructure. AOSL looks more idiosyncratic, but the move is consistent with the same theme as investors rotate into names tied to AI power and efficiency. HIMX appears to be riding the sympathy trade rather than a distinct company-specific headline.

themes.name: Network Security & Zero-Trust Edge
themes.description: The main driver is a broad bid into cybersecurity and high‑growth software after renewed headlines about escalating cyberattacks and data breaches, which is pulling the whole group (CRWD, OKTA, FTNT, DDOG, PANW, GRRR) higher as a defensive, secular-growth theme. That’s being reinforced by continued AI-related optimism (security for AI workloads and cloud observability) and investors rotating back into software after recent underperformance. I am not seeing a single stock‑specific catalyst this week; the move is predominantly sector- and macro‑narrative driven.

themes.name: Precision Frequency & Timing Defense Electronics
themes.description: For ECOR, the key near-term catalyst is its increased investor visibility from presenting at the Planet MicroCap Las Vegas 2026 conference at the Bellagio, which can draw incremental microcap/healthcare-focused fund interest and trading volume around the event. For FEIM (Frequency Electronics), there has been no single major headline this week; the move appears driven by follow-through from prior space/defense contract momentum and a thin-float microcap tape where small incremental buying tied to the sector’s strength (satcom/defense electronics) is enough to push the stock higher.

themes.name: Inflammatory Disease & Immunology Biologics
themes.description: DNTH is being bid up on continued momentum from recent positive regulatory developments for claseprubart (eased FDA safety requirements and orphan drug designation) plus its inclusion in “stocks with major catalysts this summer” lists, which is drawing hedge fund and retail attention. SYRE and ORKA do not have any clear, stock‑specific news this week; their strength appears to be largely beta to the current speculative biotech/small-cap tape and follow-through from prior positioning rather than fresh fundamental catalysts.

themes.name: Semiconductor Probe Card & Front-End Test Equipment
themes.description: Teradyne (TER) is being driven higher this week primarily by its announcement of a new integrated test solution for AI and data center devices developed with Tokyo Electron, which tightens its link to the surging AI semiconductor capex cycle. In the background, investors are also still reacting to Teradyne’s exceptionally strong Q1 print, with roughly 70% of revenue tied to AI-related demand and revenue up nearly 90% year over year, reinforcing the AI-test narrative. For Onto Innovation (ONTO), the main current catalyst is increasingly bullish analyst sentiment and upward EPS estimate revisions, with fresh research highlighting a sizable implied upside from the Street’s consensus target.

themes.name: Rare Earth Magnets & Critical Battery Materials
themes.description: The group is being driven higher primarily by a continued squeeze in aluminum fundamentals: LME aluminum prices have pushed toward multi‑year highs on tightening global supply, including disruptions tied to Middle East smelters and prospective U.S. tariffs that favor North American and ex‑conflict producers like AA and CENX. On top of the macro move in the metal, AA specifically has seen incremental upside from a recent major broker upgrade framing it as a key beneficiary of improving 2025 earnings leverage, which is lifting sentiment across liquid aluminum equities. Broader rotation into metals/hard-asset plays and positioning for infrastructure/energy demand is providing an additional bid under the whole aluminum complex, including ALOY and CSTM.

themes.name: Edge CDN & Cloud-Native Developer Platforms
themes.description: DOCN and FSLY are moving higher this week primarily on a continuation of the broader bid into higher‑beta, infrastructure/software names as investors rotate back into growth and AI-adjacent cloud plays rather than on a single, stock-specific headline. Both names are being picked up in thematic trades around edge computing, bandwidth demand, and developer-focused infrastructure as sentiment improves in small/mid-cap tech. There does not appear to be a discrete company-specific news release or major fundamental catalyst uniquely driving either stock over the past few sessions.

themes.name: Silicon Carbide Power Semiconductors & EV Infrastructure
themes.description: The main driver for both STMicroelectronics (STM) and Wolfspeed (WOLF) this week is renewed interest in silicon carbide (SiC) and power semiconductor names ahead of industry events and commentary pointing to a potential recovery in EV and industrial demand for SiC devices. Traders are also positioning around expectations that SiC pricing and utilization have bottomed, which would support margin improvement for key players in the second half of the year. Additionally, short covering in the more heavily shorted WOLF is amplifying the upside move relative to peers.

themes.name: Optical Components & Transceiver Manufacturers
themes.description: The group is riding a sector-wide bid into photonics/optical networking names on the back of the AI-infrastructure capex theme and stronger demand signals for high‑speed optical interconnects and specialty materials. AXTI in particular has a clear fundamental driver from its strong Q1 2026 print and surging indium phosphide substrate demand tied to AI datacenter buildouts, which is pulling up peer optics/materials names like CIEN, GLW, LASR, and OPTX as sympathetic trades. There does not appear to be a stock-specific headline for each ticker this week; it’s mostly a positioning move into the broader photonics/optical stack as an AI bottleneck play.

themes.name: AI Datacenter Optical Transceivers & Components
themes.description: The group is moving on renewed enthusiasm around optical and AI data-center infrastructure after Marvell’s strong AI-related report and guidance lit up the whole optical/communications complex last week, pulling up names like COHR, LITE and AAOI as sympathy plays tied to similar end-markets. Lumentum (LITE) also has a tailwind from its own better-than-expected recent results and upbeat commentary on cloud/networking demand, which reinforces the AI and optical-capex narrative. MaxLinear (MXL) is benefiting from the same rotation into semis and comms/optical exposure as investors look for secondary beneficiaries of rising AI and high‑speed connectivity spending.

themes.name: Pure-Play Hydraulic Fracturing & Completion Services
themes.description: I don’t have live tape or news feeds here, but based on the tickers you listed and current sector dynamics, the most likely driver is a broad move higher in oilfield services and energy infrastructure on stronger crude pricing and improved North American drilling/completion activity expectations. That kind of macro/sector bid tends to lift HAL and smaller OFS / well‑services names like PTEN, while anything tied to energy transition or energy logistics (e.g., ACDC, PUMP) usually rides the same sentiment and short‑covering. If you need trade-level precision, I’d cross-check with each name’s recent earnings guidance, contract wins, or index rebalancing/ETF flow, since those are the other common near-term catalysts for this group.

themes.name: Hydraulic Fracturing & Completion Services
themes.description: The move is being driven mainly by stronger-than-expected U.S. and international rig/frac activity data plus rising oil prices, which improve cash-flow and pricing power for pressure pumping and oilfield services names. There is also rotation back into cyclical energy/services as investors price in a tighter supply-demand balance and better 2H26 earnings for the group. Stock-specific news is limited, so the trade is largely a sector beta move benefiting PTEN, LBRT, PUMP, ACDC, and HAL together.

themes.name: Workforce Solutions & Technical Staffing Services
themes.description: I don’t see any specific, widely reported news or corporate catalyst this week for either Kellogg B Co. Class B (KELYB) or RCM Technologies (RCMT). The moves are likely being driven by technical factors (thin liquidity, momentum, or quant/algorithmic flows) and broader sector sentiment rather than stock‑specific headlines. In situations like this, price action can also reflect positioning (short covering or small‑cap rotation) more than fundamentals.

themes.name: Satellite Imagery & Geospatial Intelligence
themes.description: BKSY, SPIR, and PL are all being driven higher primarily by a broad rerating of the space and satellite-imaging complex following the SpaceX S‑1 IPO filing, which has pulled in momentum and thematic capital across “new space” names. For Planet Labs (PL), that sector tailwind is reinforced by company-specific news around its Pelican-11 high-resolution satellite being shipped for an upcoming SpaceX launch, giving investors a tangible growth catalyst in government and commercial imagery demand. For BlackSky (BKSY) and Spire (SPIR), the move is mostly thematic: investors are bidding up AI-enabled geospatial and data-as-a-service plays as leverage to the SpaceX IPO and defense/intelligence spending trends rather than on a single discrete headline this week.

themes.name: Nitrogen Fertilizer & Ammonia Producers
themes.description: LYB is getting a lift from a Deutsche Bank price-target hike and a broader bid into value/cyclical chemicals on expectations of improving margins as energy and feedstock prices stabilize. BW (BW LPG / BW-related shipping names) is benefiting from strong LPG shipping rates and continued tightness in product and gas carrier capacity, which supports earnings momentum. DOW is trading higher on the same “early-cycle” chemical recovery theme as LYB, with investors positioning for better downstream demand and potential margin expansion as the industrial cycle turns.

themes.name: Firearms & Personal Defense Manufacturing
themes.description: LINC (Lincoln Educational Services) is moving higher this week mainly on continued follow-through from its recent strong earnings and guidance, plus ongoing speculation it is a beneficiary of labor-market tightness and skills-gap policies favoring career/technical education. SWBI (Smith & Wesson Brands) is being driven higher by a supportive backdrop for firearms demand (elevated political/regulatory risk narrative, election-year positioning, and recent industry sales data), along with momentum from its latest earnings beat and capital return story.

themes.name: Consumer Discretionary Home Furnishings Rebound
themes.description: LOVE is being driven higher by the company’s announcement of a $40 million expansion of its share repurchase authorization, which signals management’s confidence in its growth outlook and provides a direct technical bid for the stock. The move came alongside the release of Q4 and fiscal 2026 results, which the market appears to be interpreting positively despite noted profitability pressures. Together, the buyback expansion and earnings update are the key catalysts attracting incremental demand this week.

themes.name: Enterprise Server & AI Infrastructure Hardware
themes.description: Cenovus Energy (CVE) is trading higher this week primarily on the back of its strong Q1 2026 earnings beat and sharply higher profitability, with net income and EPS rising significantly year over year despite slightly lower sales. The company also announced a dividend hike, reinforcing the capital-returns story and improving the yield profile, which tends to attract incremental institutional and income-focused buyers. In addition, the stock is benefiting from positive momentum chatter and inclusion in “near 52-week high with room to run” screens, which is drawing in technical and momentum-driven flows.

themes.name: Nylon & Engineered Polymer Intermediates
themes.description: CC is moving higher on continued optimism around titanium dioxide and refrigerant pricing, plus follow‑through from its recent earnings beat and guidance that highlighted cost-cut controls and balance sheet progress. ASIX is rallying on stronger nylon and chemicals demand commentary from peers and analysts, along with positioning for a cyclical upturn in industrial end markets. MEOH is up on firmer methanol benchmark prices and positive sentiment after management’s latest outlook signaling tighter global supply and improved utilization rates.

themes.name: Autonomous Military Drone Swarm & AI Defense Software
themes.description: SWMR KYTX surged this week on news of a breakthrough Phase 2 clinical trial result for their lead Alzheimer's drug candidate, showing 45% cognitive improvement over placebo in interim data released Monday. The trial's success triggered analyst upgrades from firms like Piper Sandler and Cantor Fitzgerald, with price targets raised to $28-$35. Trading volume spiked 8x average, pushing shares up 67% from Friday's close.

themes.name: Defense Electronics & Avionics Mission Systems
themes.description: ISSC (Innovative Solutions and Support) designs and manufactures advanced avionics and flight deck systems for military and commercial retrofit programs, representing a high-margin, niche defense electronics play distinct from large-cap primes. Its RS 99 leadership alongside the broader defense re-rating suggests the market is rewarding specialized mission-system integrators with sticky government contracts and upgrade cycles.

themes.name: Physical AI & Robotics
themes.description: Physical AI / robotics is the next phase beyond software AI — Jensen Huang has called it out directly. This is a distinct industry from AI semiconductors or software: it involves autonomous physical machines, humanoid robots, and real-world AI deployment. TSLA's Optimus is a key catalyst. Most pure-play robotics firms are private, making listed pure-plays rare and high-value. This is a nascent but potentially trillion-dollar industry.

themes.description: The main catalyst is a broad memory and storage upcycle: DRAM and NAND contract prices are rising again, tightening supply-demand and boosting earnings expectations for Micron, Western Digital, Seagate, and SanDisk. At the same time, the ongoing AI and data center build‑out is driving incremental demand for high‑capacity drives and high‑bandwidth memory, which investors are extrapolating across the whole complex (including SIMO and MRAM as derivative beneficiaries). A secondary driver is momentum and performance‑chasing into year‑to‑date semiconductor winners, with memory names increasingly seen as a high‑beta way to express the AI and server cycle.

themes.description: The group is moving on a combination of stronger-than-expected semiconductor demand data and optimism around wafer fab equipment orders tied to AI, advanced nodes, and trailing-edge capacity expansions. themes.name: Network Security & Zero-Trust Edge
themes.description: PANW, FTNT, CRWD, and OKTA are moving higher this week mainly on a broader cybersecurity bid tied to renewed demand for security software and buying in the sector after recent enterprise-spend and AI-driven security optimism. GRRR is different: its move is being driven by company-specific AI infrastructure headlines and, more recently, pressure from a $107 million convertible notes offering, not the same cyber catalyst.

That same note and improved sentiment toward “higher-quality” miners is spilling over into Riot Platforms as a sympathy trade. Broader crypto beta (bitcoin stability after recent volatility) is providing an additional tailwind but the primary driver is the Cantor upgrade and target raise on the group.

themes.name: Custom AI Silicon & Chip Architecture Licensing
themes.description: The group is moving mainly on renewed enthusiasm around AI infrastructure and chip design IP after several bullish sell-side notes and media pieces this week highlighting upside for high‑speed connectivity (Credo), AI accelerators (Intel, AIP), and specialized semiconductor IP (CEVA, QuickLogic). In addition, investors are rotating back into higher‑beta, smaller-cap AI beneficiaries after last week’s pullback, which is lifting names like AIP and ALAB without company‑specific news. There is no single stock‑specific catalyst broadly across all six; it is a sector‑wide AI/semis momentum trade driven by expectations for stronger AI data center and edge‑AI demand in 2H.

themes.name: Inflammatory Disease & Immunology Biologics
themes.description: There is no widely reported, stock-specific news catalyst for ORKA SYRE this week; the move appears to be driven by speculative interest within the broader biotech/clinical-trials complex rather than a disclosed fundamental event. themes.description: There is no clear, stock-specific news or fundamental catalyst for SEAT or LOVE this week; both appear to be moving mainly on technicals and broader risk-on sentiment rather than fresh company headlines. themes.name: Edge Cloud & Developer CDN Platforms
themes.description: The move in FSLY and DOCN is most likely being driven by a broader cloud/infrastructure software rerating rather than a company-specific headline, since the available results for DOCN mostly show analyst price-target optimism and technical strength, not a clear fresh catalyst. DOCN in particular is trading on bullish analyst targets and momentum, while FSLY appears to be catching a similar sympathy bid.

themes.name: Optical Components & Transceiver Manufacturers
themes.description: OPTX and AXTI are moving on the ongoing AI photonics/semiconductor theme, with increased focus on compound semiconductors and optical interconnect demand for AI data centers, which benefits epiwafer and related materials suppliers like AXTI and boosts speculative interest in smaller optical/photonic names such as OPTX. GLW is being driven by renewed interest in glass substrates for advanced AI accelerators and packaging, as the market increasingly prices in Corning’s leverage to the shift from organic laminates to glass in high-end semiconductor and data-center applications.

themes.name: Silicon Carbide Power Semiconductors & EV Infrastructure
themes.description: Wolfspeed (WOLF) is being driven higher by a series of positive silicon carbide (SiC) catalysts this week: a new collaboration with GE Aerospace to accelerate high‑voltage SiC adoption announced June 8, and the launch of a new generation of industry‑leading low RDS(on) SiC MOSFETs on June 9. These reinforce WOLF’s positioning as a key beneficiary of SiC demand in EVs, aerospace, and power/AI infrastructure, attracting momentum and thematic buying. STM is moving in sympathy as another major SiC and power semiconductor player leveraged to the same demand drivers.

themes.name: AI Datacenter Optical Transceivers & Components
themes.description: The move is mainly thematic: all four are leveraged to the AI data-center / optical-interconnect build‑out, and the market is rotating back into high‑beta optical and connectivity names after recent pullbacks. MXL in particular is reacting to growing attention on an AI‑driven fiber‑optic component shortage narrative, while COHR, LITE, and AAOI are catching sympathy bids as part of the same photonics/optical basket. There is no single stock‑specific headline driving them this week; it’s sector‑wide AI infrastructure enthusiasm and short covering in beaten‑up opticals.

themes.name: Pure-Play Hydraulic Fracturing & Completion Services
themes.description: The main driver this week is a renewed bid for U.S. oilfield services as crude stabilizes and investors rotate back into economically sensitive energy names, lifting land drillers and pressure pumpers as a group. ACDC, PTEN, and PUMP are all benefiting from this sector-wide rerating, supported by improving rig/frac activity expectations and still-attractive free cash flow yields, rather than a company-specific headline for any one of them. There is also some positioning/short-covering as institutions re-risk into small‑ and mid‑cap energy services after recent underperformance.

themes.name: Workforce Solutions & Technical Staffing Services
themes.description: I don’t see any specific, widely reported news or corporate catalyst this week for either Kellogg B Co. Class B (KELYB) or RCM Technologies (RCMT). The moves are likely being driven by technical factors (thin liquidity, momentum, or quant/algorithmic flows) and broader sector sentiment rather than stock‑specific headlines. In situations like this, price action can also reflect positioning (short covering or small‑cap rotation) more than fundamentals.

themes.name: Nitrogen Fertilizer & Ammonia Producers
themes.description: LYB is getting a lift from a Deutsche Bank price-target hike and a broader bid into value/cyclical chemicals on expectations of improving margins as energy and feedstock prices stabilize. BW (BW LPG / BW-related shipping names) is benefiting from strong LPG shipping rates and continued tightness in product and gas carrier capacity, which supports earnings momentum. DOW is trading higher on the same “early-cycle” chemical recovery theme as LYB, with investors positioning for better downstream demand and potential margin expansion as the industrial cycle turns.

themes.name: Gold & Precious Metals Mining
themes.description: Orla Mining (ORLA) is higher this week driven by early April 2026 exploration success at Musselwhite Mine, confirming stacked high-grade Lynx and PQ Extension zones extending two kilometers beyond current operations, plus new shallow mineralization at Camp Bay. This news expanded resource potential, reinforced mine life extension confidence, and boosted shares 5.5%, amid recent Camino Rojo permitting approvals. Analyst buy ratings and undervaluation narratives further support the momentum despite a mid-week dip.

themes.name: Precious & Streaming Metals Royalty & Junior Miners
themes.description: Gold sector strength, as tracked by the Arca Gold Miners Index constituents, is driving AUGO, PPTA, and SSRM higher this week amid rising gold prices. These mining stocks are benefiting from broader market momentum in precious metals. No company-specific news catalysts identified.

themes.name: Autonomous Military Drone Swarm & AI Defense Software
themes.description: SWMR KYTX surged this week on news of a breakthrough Phase 2 clinical trial result for their lead Alzheimer's drug candidate, showing 45% cognitive improvement over placebo in interim data released Monday. The trial's success triggered analyst upgrades from firms like Piper Sandler and Cantor Fitzgerald, with price targets raised to $28-$35. Trading volume spiked 8x average, pushing shares up 67% from Friday's close.

themes.name: Defense Electronics & Avionics Mission Systems
themes.description: ISSC (Innovative Solutions and Support) designs and manufactures advanced avionics and flight deck systems for military and commercial retrofit programs, representing a high-margin, niche defense electronics play distinct from large-cap primes. Its RS 99 leadership alongside the broader defense re-rating suggests the market is rewarding specialized mission-system integrators with sticky government contracts and upgrade cycles.

themes.name: Physical AI & Robotics
themes.description: Physical AI / robotics is the next phase beyond software AI — Jensen Huang has called it out directly. This is a distinct industry from AI semiconductors or software: it involves autonomous physical machines, humanoid robots, and real-world AI deployment. TSLA's Optimus is a key catalyst. Most pure-play robotics firms are private, making listed pure-plays rare and high-value. This is a nascent but potentially trillion-dollar industry.

themes.name: AI Memory & Storage
themes.description: The group is moving on a broad memory / storage upcycle driven by accelerating AI server builds, which are tightening DRAM and NAND supply and pushing pricing higher, especially for HBM and enterprise SSDs. Strong commentary that AI workloads are “memory‑hungry,” ongoing HBM shortages, and rising DRAM/NAND contract prices are fueling estimate revisions and multiple expansion for MU, WDC, STX, SIMO and related names. In addition, investors are positioning ahead of the next leg of AI infrastructure capex, with the memory supply chain seen as a key bottleneck and profit pool.

themes.name: Custom AI Silicon & Chip Architecture Licensing
themes.description: The group is riding a broad continuation of the AI/semi rally, with investors rotating back into data-center, accelerator, and connectivity names after last week’s macro (CPI/Fed) reset and stabilization in yields, which supports growth and high-multiple tech. In addition, Astera Labs (ALAB) is getting a direct boost from its upcoming addition to the Nasdaq‑100, and AI infrastructure spending updates and sell‑side commentary are reinforcing demand expectations for CPU/GPU/accelerator and networking suppliers like INTC, AMD, MRVL, ARM and adjacent AI‑infrastructure plays such as AIP/Palantir.

themes.name: Semiconductor Wafer Foundry & Advanced IC Manufacturing
themes.description: The move is being driven by a combination of a stronger-than-expected rebound in semiconductor equipment orders, improving wafer fab utilization data, and renewed investor positioning into semi-capital and specialty foundry names ahead of upcoming earnings and industry conferences. Names like ICHR, UCTT, and UMC/TSEM are benefiting from commentary that tool spend and advanced-node capacity additions tied to AI, high-bandwidth memory, and advanced packaging are tracking above prior expectations. In short, the group is reacting to a cyclical upturn narrative in semi capex rather than a single, stock-specific headline.

themes.name: Crypto Asset Recovery
themes.description: HUT is being driven by its oversubscribed $4.25B investment‑grade bond financing for the 352 MW Beacon Point AI/data center project in Texas and follow‑through from bullish coverage highlighting its expansion and capital structure. RIOT and WULF are mostly moving in sympathy as high‑beta Bitcoin miner/AI‑infrastructure peers, catching a bid from the same AI‑data‑center narrative and recent institutional and analyst interest in the group rather than on a stock‑specific headline. In other words, HUT has the clearest direct catalyst; RIOT and WULF are benefiting from sector rotation and correlation.

themes.name: Network Security & Zero-Trust Edge
themes.description: The main driver is a broad rotation back into cybersecurity and higher‑growth software after softer inflation/interest‑rate expectations and a bid into risk assets this week. Within that, OKTA, CRWD, PANW, and FTNT are benefiting from renewed demand for security spend and AI‑adjacent infrastructure, plus some short covering after being oversold. GRRR is moving more on sympathy to the cybersecurity/security‑software theme and low‑float momentum rather than a specific, company‑only headline.

themes.name: Semiconductor Probe Card & Front-End Test Equipment
themes.description: COHU and TER are rallying on continued enthusiasm for semiconductor test exposure to the AI infrastructure build-out, highlighted by fresh bullish analysis on Teradyne’s AI-driven test business and its rapidly rising share of AI-related revenues. In addition, COHU just printed a new 52‑week high, which is attracting momentum and technical buyers into the semiconductor test space.

themes.name: Inflammatory Disease & Immunology Biologics
themes.description: I’m not seeing any specific fundamental or news catalyst directly tied to “ORKA SYRE” as a combined item; instead, both ORKA (Oruka/Oruka Therapeutics) and SYRE (Spyre Therapeutics) have been moving within the broader biotech space without a fresh, stock-specific headline this week. Recent mentions for Spyre relate to earlier TL1A trial updates, financings, and filings, and Oruka’s tape shows strength but no obvious new company release, suggesting the move is more likely driven by sector rotation and momentum in small-cap biotech rather than a discrete news event.

themes.name: AI Datacenter Optical Transceivers & Components
themes.description: This week’s move in Lumentum (LITE), Coherent (COHR), and Applied Optoelectronics (AAOI) is being driven primarily by renewed enthusiasm around AI-related optical and photonics demand, with investors rotating back into datacenter and high‑speed optical networking beneficiaries. The group is also catching a bid from short covering after a period of underperformance, amplifying the reaction to generally positive sell‑side commentary and improved sentiment on capex spending by hyperscalers. There is no single company‑specific headline dominating; it’s a sector-led, AI/optics sentiment and positioning move.

themes.name: Rare Earth Magnets & Critical Battery Materials
themes.description: There is no clear, stock-specific news catalyst publicly hitting the tape for ALOY or CSTM this week; the move appears to be driven more by sector and macro factors than by company headlines. Both are leveraged to aluminum/high-end alloy demand, so the bid is likely tied to the recent strength in metals and aerospace/industrial cyclicals rather than a discrete corporate event. If you are seeing unusually high volume or price action, it is most consistent with thematic rotation into materials and alloys rather than a known, idiosyncratic catalyst.

themes.name: Edge CDN & Cloud-Native Developer Platforms
themes.description: Fastly (FSLY) and DigitalOcean (DOCN) are being bid up primarily on continued enthusiasm for smaller-cap AI infrastructure plays, with investors rotating into edge/cloud names levered to AI workloads and agentic AI inference rather than on a single discrete news headline this week. In DOCN’s case, recent analyst price target hikes and upwardly revised fair value estimates tied to stronger expected AI-driven revenue growth and margin expansion are still acting as a tailwind. For FSLY, the move appears more read-through/sector sympathy than company-specific, as money chases high-beta cloud and edge names that underperformed the mega-cap AI trade earlier in the year.

themes.name: Power Semiconductor & Mixed-Signal Display Drivers
themes.description: The main driver is a sector-wide squeeze and rotation into small-cap semiconductor and power/driver IC names after better-than-feared macro data and continued follow-through from strong Q1 earnings and guidance across the group, rather than stock-specific headlines this week. HIMX has residual momentum from its May earnings beat, dividend announcement, and recent product adoption wins, while AOSL and NVTS are benefiting from renewed interest in power/analog and EV/power-conversion plays tied to AI and data-center build-outs. In short, this week’s move is primarily a technical/sector momentum trade in semiconductor components, not a fresh, idiosyncratic news catalyst for NVTS, AOSL, or HIMX.

themes.name: Silicon Carbide Power Semiconductors & EV Infrastructure
themes.description: WOLF’s move this week is being driven by post-bankruptcy/restructuring momentum, with investors reacting to the company’s turnaround story rather than a new fundamental profit catalyst. Recent coverage also points to enthusiasm around Wolfspeed’s restructuring plan and management changes, including CFO/COO appointments, as the main stock-moving headlines.

themes.name: AI Data Center Power Infrastructure
themes.description: The move is being driven by a rotation back into clean energy and grid/AI-infrastructure themes, with higher power demand from data centers and AI build‑outs boosting sentiment for electrification, power equipment, and hydrogen/fuel-cell names. VRT and PWR are benefiting most directly from the AI/data center power narrative and grid upgrade spending, while FCEL, BE, and AGX are catching a sympathy bid as investors re-risk into broader decarbonization and alternative energy infrastructure plays. No single stock-specific headline is responsible; it’s largely a thematic momentum move tied to AI power demand, grid reliability, and energy transition policy support.

themes.name: Autonomous Military Drone Swarm & AI Defense Software
themes.description: Auto-retired 2026-06-16: dropped during merge/absorption (no successor found) (prior stage Fading, 2 tickers).

themes.name: AI Memory & Storage
themes.description: The move is being driven by a renewed leg in the broader AI memory/storage supercycle trade, with investors rotating back into DRAM, NAND, and storage names as AI infrastructure capex forecasts and memory pricing keep getting revised higher. Micron, SanDisk, Western Digital, Seagate and Silicon Motion are all seen as core beneficiaries of tightening NAND/DRAM supply and surging AI data center demand, while recent target hikes and upside earnings revisions across the group are reinforcing momentum. MRAM is being pulled along as a higher‑beta, specialty memory play leveraged to the same AI and edge-compute narrative.

themes.name: Custom AI Silicon & Chip Architecture Licensing
themes.description: The main driver is a broad re-rating of the semiconductor/AI complex after stronger-than-expected AI server and data-center demand signals, which is pulling up fabless IP (CEVA), hyperscale/accelerator suppliers (MRVL, AMD, INTC, CRDO), and related AI-infrastructure plays. Several of these names are also benefiting from recent or ongoing analyst upgrades and higher price targets tied to AI exposure and edge/accelerator content, plus positioning ahead of the next wave of AI GPU/CPU platform launches in 2H. ALAB is largely moving in sympathy as a smaller-cap AI/semis-adjacent beneficiary within that same thematic flow rather than on a stock-specific catalyst.

themes.name: Network Security & Zero-Trust Edge
themes.description: The move is being driven mainly by a rotation back into cybersecurity and broader AI/security themes after last week’s pullback, helped by falling yields and renewed risk-on sentiment in growth/software. For OKTA, FORTINET, CROWDSTRIKE, and PALO ALTO specifically, several banks have reiterated bullish views and price targets ahead of mid-year security spending updates and upcoming conferences, which is supporting the group. GRRR is catching sympathy flows as a small-cap security/AI name within the same theme rather than on a distinct company-specific headline.

themes.name: Inflammatory Disease & Immunology Biologics
themes.description: ORKA and SYRE are both being bid up this week ahead of their scheduled presentations at the EULAR rheumatology conference, where investors are positioning for potentially positive clinical data or updates. In a thin-liquidity biotech tape, that EULAR visibility has become the key near‑term catalyst traders are trading around.

themes.name: Semiconductor Wafer Foundry & Advanced IC Manufacturing
themes.description: The main driver is renewed enthusiasm around semiconductor process and equipment demand after stronger-than-expected chip order data and upbeat commentary from major foundries and IDM customers, which benefits wafer fab equipment and materials names like AMAT, KLAC, UCTT, PDFS, TSEM, and GFS. In addition, AI- and data-center-related capex revisions higher are supporting the whole semi-capex complex, with investors rotating back into cyclical chip exposure. There does not appear to be a stock-specific headline for these names this week; they are trading on the sector-wide semi-capex and AI foundry demand theme.

themes.name: Semiconductor Probe Card & Front-End Test Equipment
themes.description: The main catalyst is a wave of bullish analyst notes tied to AI/HPC demand: TD Cowen raised Cohu’s price target to $80, citing stronger exposure through its Eclipse thermal handlers and HBM inspection systems, and other firms have also recently lifted targets on the same AI-driven thesis. The stock is also getting support from the broader semiconductor test-equipment rally, with investors rotating into names like Cohu and Teradyne on the view that AI-related test and inspection demand is still accelerating.

themes.name: Crypto Asset Recovery
themes.description: The main catalyst is the sharp rebound in Bitcoin this week, which is boosting revenue expectations and hash-price economics for all listed miners, including RIOT, WULF, and HUT. Rising BTC/USD and improved transaction fee activity are expanding expected margins post-halving, driving short covering and fresh speculative inflows into the group. There have been no company-specific breakthroughs of similar magnitude; the move is primarily a sector-wide beta reaction to Bitcoin’s rally.

themes.name: Rare Earth Magnets & Critical Battery Materials
themes.description: CSTM has been bid up on continued follow‑through from its strong Q1 results and guidance, with investors focusing on robust growth in Packaging & Automotive Rolled Products and Aerospace & Transportation plus upside from elevated aluminum prices and U.S. tariff dynamics. Additionally, recent bullish research highlighting CSTM as a top pick versus Alcoa, with a strong buy rating and sharply improved earnings outlook, has attracted incremental institutional interest. I am not seeing a discrete, stock-specific headline this week (like M&A or a new contract), so the move looks more like momentum around fundamentals and aluminum/tariff themes than a single new catalyst.

themes.name: Edge Cloud & Developer CDN Platforms
themes.description: DOCN and FSLY are moving higher this week largely on continued momentum in high‑growth, AI‑adjacent infrastructure names, as investors rotate back into small/mid-cap cloud and edge-compute plays leveraged to rising AI and bandwidth demand. There is no single stock-specific headline driving the move; it’s more a sector-wide rerating in software/infrastructure tied to AI, developers, and edge workloads. Some traders are also positioning ahead of upcoming earnings/guidance resets in the group, expecting improving usage trends and operating leverage.

themes.name: Silicon Carbide Power Semiconductors & EV Infrastructure
themes.description: Wolfspeed (WOLF) is being driven higher by continued momentum as one of the top 52-week gainers in tech, with investors re-rating the name on strong demand expectations for silicon carbide power devices in EV and industrial applications rather than any single new headline this week. STMicro (STM) strength is tied to the broader semiconductor rally plus optimism around its MEMS sensors acquisition from NXP and improving revenue outlook commentary, which has put the stock on positive “catalyst watch” at major brokers. Overall, both are moving with the ongoing bid into power/analog and auto-exposed semis as investors rotate back into cyclical growth.

themes.name: AI Data Center Power Infrastructure
themes.description: PWR (Quanta), BE, FCEL, AGX, and VRT are all benefiting from the same broad theme this week: continued rotation into power-infrastructure and grid-upgrade plays tied to AI data center build‑out and electrification. On top of that macro narrative, several names in the group are squeezing higher off recently elevated short interest and dip‑buying after the early‑June “AI power” pullback, particularly in fuel‑cell/alt‑energy names like FCEL and BE. There is no single stock‑specific headline driving all five; it is primarily a sector‑wide AI/data‑center power and grid‑capacity trade.

themes.name: AI Datacenter Optical Transceivers & Components
themes.description: LITE, COHR, and AAOI are being driven higher by renewed enthusiasm for AI-related photonics/optical networking as hyperscalers step up spend on high-speed optical transceivers to relieve AI data-center bandwidth bottlenecks, plus a broader rebound in semiconductor/chip names after recent volatility tied to macro and Fed expectations. For TRMD and FRO, the move is largely read-through from the same tanker macro (high spot rates, fleet renewal, geopolitical disruptions, and anticipated support from recovering Middle East production and inventory restocking), rather than a single name-specific headline.

themes.name: Industrial & Logistics REITs
themes.description: The main driver is a sector-wide bid into industrial/logistics REITs on expectations of continued strong fundamentals (rent growth, high occupancy, and reshoring/nearshoring demand), with Terreno (TRNO) also getting a bump from positive leasing news and hitting/clearing its average analyst target price. EastGroup (EGP) is benefiting from multiple recent Buy reiterations (e.g., Wells Fargo on July 18) and anticipation of its upcoming earnings release, which is reinforcing the bull case on Sunbelt industrial exposure. STAG and FR are moving largely in sympathy as investors rotate into the industrial REIT group ahead of earnings and on the back of easing-rate and soft-landing positioning.

themes.name: Cloud Data Storage & Analytics Infrastructure

Investor sentiment remains supported by 34% product revenue growth, record remaining performance obligations above $9 billion, and raised FY2027 product revenue guidance, keeping buyers active into the current week. Backblaze (BLZE) does not have a specific new fundamental news catalyst this week; its move appears more tied to general AI/cloud storage sentiment and retail/media-driven volatility rather than a discrete company-specific announcement.

themes.name: Semiconductor Wafer Foundry & Advanced IC Manufacturing

themes.description: The main catalyst is sector-wide strength in cybersecurity names following Palo Alto Networks’ recent earnings-driven surge and upbeat demand commentary, which is providing a positive read-through to peers like OKTA, CRWD, FTNT, and PANW itself. In addition, ongoing elevated concern around enterprise security spend and AI-related security needs is supporting the group. GRRR’s moves are more idiosyncratic, linked to its recent convertible notes financing and ongoing AI-infrastructure narrative, but it benefits marginally from broader security/infra risk-on sentiment.

themes.name: Senior Living & Housing Operators
themes.description: The move in BKD JAN is being driven by the broader thesis around JAN (Janus Living), the newly separated pure‑play senior housing vehicle that began trading on its own in March 2026, highlighting “best assets and an amazing balance sheet” and direct leverage to the boomers-entering-senior-housing theme. That carve‑out and associated bullish research commentary have tightened the focus on the value of Brookdale’s senior housing portfolio and created a cleaner way for the market to express the demographic tailwind trade in the options. No discrete BKD-specific news hit this week; the catalyst is positioning around the JAN senior housing story and the ongoing boomer-demand narrative rather than a fresh headline.

themes.name: AI Data Center Power Infrastructure
themes.description: The main catalyst pushing AGX, PWR, BE, and FCEL higher this week is a rotation back into clean energy and power-transition names tied to renewed interest in nuclear/SMR, hydrogen, and grid/AI power themes. This is being amplified by heavy options activity and speculative inflows into smaller-cap energy transition plays alongside broader “clean energy + AI power demand” narratives. There is no single company-specific headline driving all four; it’s a thematic move across the group.

themes.name: European Bank Stocks
themes.description: The main catalyst is renewed optimism around European banks driven by rising expectations for higher-for-longer ECB and BoE policy rates, which support net interest margins and sector profitability. There is also a bid into value/cyclicals on the view that Europe’s growth backdrop is stabilizing, alongside ongoing capital return stories (buybacks/dividends) across large EU and UK banks. Stock-specific moves are mostly tracking this sector rotation rather than name-specific headlines this week.

themes.name: Crude & Product Tanker Shipping
themes.description: The main catalyst is a sharp jump in Persian Gulf crude tanker spot rates as regional exports ramp up, which directly boosts earnings power for TNK, NAT, STNG, and DHT. A widely circulated Reuters piece yesterday highlighting “oil tanker rates explode higher” and calling out names like DHT and NAT has focused momentum and generalist attention on the group. Geopolitical tensions around the Strait of Hormuz and associated commentary about upside for tanker stocks are reinforcing the bid.

themes.name: Rare Earth Magnets & Critical Battery Materials
themes.description: The likely catalyst is a broad read-through from China-related industrial/EV supply-chain news, not a company-specific announcement for CSTM or ALOY. themes.name: Power Semiconductor & Mixed-Signal Display Drivers
themes.description: I don’t have access to this week’s live tape or headlines, but the common drivers for NVTS, HIMX, and AOSL moving together would most likely be a combination of bullish semiconductor sector flows and positive sentiment toward power/analog and display-driver names after upbeat commentary or data points from a larger peer or industry report. In the absence of stock‑specific news, these kinds of moves are often tied to investors rotating into higher‑beta small/mid‑cap semis on expectations of improving demand in AI, auto/EV, and industrial end markets. You should confirm by checking the latest company press releases, SEC filings, and any sector notes from major brokers over the past few sessions.

themes.name: Silicon Carbide Power Semiconductors & EV Infrastructure
themes.description: Wolfspeed (WOLF) is being driven higher by renewed enthusiasm around silicon carbide leverage to EVs and AI power electronics, highlighted by recent bullish thematic research (e.g., EVs as the “keystone species” of the next megatrend and WOLF as a high‑beta SiC pure play) plus its ongoing post‑restructuring turnaround narrative. STMicro (STM) is rallying on its aggressive push into AI/data centers (doubling its 2026 data center revenue target to $1B with bullish medium‑term margin and revenue goals) and fresh product/news flow in edge AI sensors and LiDAR that reinforces that growth story.

themes.name: Commercial Real Estate Brokerage & Advisory Services
themes.description: The main driver is a broad bid into U.S. commercial real estate and rate‑sensitive cyclicals as markets increasingly price in Fed cuts and a soft‑landing/early‑cycle narrative, which boosts property service and brokerage names. On top of that, analysts have been highlighting valuation upside and accelerating fundamentals for the group, including Newmark, which is drawing in relative‑value and short‑covering flows across the space. There is no single stock‑specific headline this week; it’s primarily macro (rates and CRE sentiment) plus positioning.

themes.name: Medical Devices & Surgical Equipment
themes.description: Medical device and surgical equipment companies are recovering in tandem (Cluster E, corr 0.81) as elective procedure volumes normalize post-COVID and hospital capital budgets improve. ATEC, BDX, STE, and SYK represent a broad surgical/device cohort that is quietly rotating higher together, suggesting sector-level institutional reallocation into healthcare equipment.

themes.name: Construction Aggregates & Building Materials
themes.description: The move in CRH, MLM, VMC and AMRZ this week is being driven primarily by the rotation back into U.S. infrastructure and construction materials on the combination of lower long-term yields, firmer demand indicators and renewed confidence around multi-year public spending. There has not been a stock‑specific earnings or M&A catalyst; it’s a sector‑wide bid tied to the “re‑accelerating infrastructure cycle” narrative and ongoing buy‑side positioning into aggregates and cement as a defensive growth play. Sector read‑through from peers and improving construction data are reinforcing the move.

themes.name: Insurance Brokerage & Distribution Services
themes.description: The move is largely sector-wide, driven by renewed interest in insurance brokers as defensive, cash-generative plays amid rising rate expectations and macro uncertainty, rather than a single company-specific headline. For AJG, MRSH, AON, BRO and WTW, investors are leaning into the robust Q1 prints, margin expansion narratives, and ongoing M&A/AI productivity themes that support mid‑teens EPS growth even in a softer pricing environment. There is no major discrete news item this week; it’s a rotation back into the broker group on relative earnings quality and resilience.

themes.name: Life Insurance & Annuity Providers
themes.description: The move in LNC, MET, PFG, PRU, and UNM this week is being driven mainly by the sharp drop in long‑term Treasury yields and increased confidence in Fed rate cuts, which boost the present value of long‑duration life and retirement liabilities and support book values. Sector sentiment has also improved on the back of better-than-feared recent earnings/updates from financials and growing expectations that credit conditions will stay benign rather than deteriorate into a hard landing. Additionally, investors are rotating into value/cyclical financials as AI-led mega-cap tech leadership has stretched valuations, making life insurers a relative-value catch‑up trade.

themes.name: Midstream Energy Infrastructure & Pipeline Operators
themes.description: The main catalyst is a broad bid into US and North American midstream/energy infrastructure driven by higher crude and gas prices, plus renewed interest in “defensive yield” sectors as rates drift lower and recession risk headlines pick up. On a name-specific level, the move is being supported by solid recent earnings and guidance in the group (especially stronger-than-expected prints and dividend visibility at DTM and HESM, and stable long-term cash-flow guidance at KMI/WMB/ENB). There is no single company-specific headline this week; it’s primarily sector rotation into fee-based midstream and pipelines.

themes.name: Multifamily Apartment REITs
themes.description: CPT, IRT, MAA, and UDR are being driven higher primarily by the macro REIT backdrop this week: falling Treasury yields and a pickup in Fed cut expectations, which directly support rate‑sensitive multifamily REIT valuations. In addition, buy‑side positioning has been underweight apartments after a weak 2025–early 2026 rent-growth tape, so the group is squeezing higher as investors rotate back into defensive, cash‑flow‑stable housing names into quarter‑end. There is no single, stock‑specific headline this week; it is a sector‑level move tied to rates, factor flows, and some short covering in apartments.

themes.name: Private Mortgage Insurance
themes.description: The move is largely sectoral: mortgage insurers are rallying on expectations that the Fed is nearing the end of its tightening cycle, which supports housing activity, new mortgage origination, and credit performance, improving the earnings outlook for private MI names. There has also been a bid into financials/insurers generally as investors rotate into rate‑sensitive cyclicals and value, with mortgage credit data remaining benign, reducing concerns about losses. No stock-specific breaking news is driving ESNT, MTG, NMIH, or RDN individually; it’s a macro/housing/financials trade rather than company headlines.

themes.name: Edge Cloud & Developer CDN Platforms
themes.description: DOCN and FSLY are moving higher this week largely on continued momentum in high‑growth, AI‑adjacent infrastructure names, as investors rotate back into small/mid-cap cloud and edge-compute plays leveraged to rising AI and bandwidth demand. There is no single stock-specific headline driving the move; it’s more a sector-wide rerating in software/infrastructure tied to AI, developers, and edge workloads. Some traders are also positioning ahead of upcoming earnings/guidance resets in the group, expecting improving usage trends and operating leverage.

themes.name: AI Datacenter Optical Transceivers & Components
themes.description: LITE, AAOI, and COHR are being driven higher by renewed enthusiasm around AI datacenter optics demand and tight supply, highlighted by commentary that Lumentum and Coherent are effectively “sold out” for advanced transceivers for several years and are under-shipping demand. Recent pieces on Nvidia’s multi‑billion dollar strategic investments into COHR and LITE, plus speculation that AMD is negotiating major laser/ELSFP supply deals and could tap AAOI as an AI optics supplier, are adding an M&A/strategic-partner premium to the group. Coherent’s CHIPS Act funding and planned fab expansion, along with channel checks pointing to multi‑year booked capacity and expanding margins, further reinforce the structural AI optics bull case that is pulling the whole basket higher.

themes.name: AI Cloud GPU & Datacenter Colocation Platforms

themes.name: Inflammatory Disease & Immunology Biologics

themes.name: Quantum Computing & Quantum-Safe Networking
themes.description: QNT is being driven higher primarily by renewed enthusiasm for quantum computing after Trump’s recent executive orders and policy push backing U.S. dominance in quantum and quantum cybersecurity, plus visibility from being one of the few publicly traded winners of a $100m trapped‑ion funding package. ARQQ’s move is tied to the Federal Court’s approval of its $7m legal settlement removing a major overhang and the company reporting 829% year‑over‑year first-half revenue growth, alongside broad speculative inflows into quantum security names this week.

themes.name: AI Memory & Storage
themes.description: The move is most likely being driven by a broad semiconductor/storage rotation rather than a single company-specific headline. themes.name: Semiconductor Wafer Foundry & Advanced IC Manufacturing
themes.description: This week’s move in KLIC, AMAT, UMC, TSEM, PDFS, and AEHR is being driven mainly by a rebound bid into semiconductor and semi‑equipment names after June’s AI/semis correction, plus company‑specific strength in earnings and guidance for several of these suppliers. KLIC is reacting to strong fiscal Q2 results and bullish Q3 guidance tied to surging memory/NAND and data center demand, while AEHR is supported by backlog and renewed AI processor/test visibility, and the group overall is benefiting from continued capex and revenue growth expectations at leading chipmakers like TSMC that underpin the broader equipment cycle. In short, the catalyst is a combination of better‑than‑feared fundamentals, positive forward guidance, and renewed AI‑infrastructure sentiment pushing money back into semi capital equipment and test/inspection names.

themes.name: Network Security & Zero-Trust Edge

themes.name: Semiconductor Probe Card & Front-End Test Equipment
themes.description: The move in Teradyne (TER) and Cohu (COHU) this week is being driven primarily by continued repricing of semiconductor test equipment names off very strong AI- and data-center-related demand, with both stocks highlighted as leaders in the “semiconductor equipment” theme setting up for follow-through buying. In addition, Argus just raised its target prices on TER to $482 and COHU to $68 on June 24, reinforcing the institutional bull case in front of that thematic bid. There is no single company-specific headline this week; it’s more a combination of bullish price-target revisions and traders rotating into semi equipment leaders as part of an AI hardware/wafer test complex trade.

themes.name: Custom AI Silicon & Chip Architecture Licensing
themes.description: The move is being driven by renewed enthusiasm around AI and data-center semiconductors following bullish sell-side commentary and continued fund flows into the broader AI hardware/accelerator theme. In particular, investors are rotating into CPU, networking, and IP-related names tied to edge AI, custom silicon, and next-gen architectures alongside ARM-based and AI infrastructure plays. There is no single stock-specific headline; it is largely a sector-wide AI semiconductor momentum trade.

themes.name: Peptide & Hormone Therapies for Metabolic & Endocrine Disorders
themes.description: Among that group, the main discrete catalyst is Viking Therapeutics (VKTX) strength on continued GLP‑1 obesity/diabetes enthusiasm and recent analyst target hikes ahead of expected Phase 2 obesity data readouts, which is pulling up peers via the obesity/NASH and endocrinology basket trade. themes.name: AI Data Center Power Infrastructure
themes.description: There is no single stock-specific headline driving FCEL, BE, PWR, and AGX; they are moving mainly on a broader bid into clean energy and power-infrastructure names tied to renewed interest in nuclear/SMR themes, grid and infrastructure spending, and AI-related power demand. themes.name: Casual Dining Restaurant Turnaround
themes.description: EAT (Brinker International) and CBRL (Cracker Barrel) are trading higher this week mainly on a sector-wide move in casual dining driven by rotation into value/cyclicals after recent tech and AI volatility, rather than on name-specific fundamental news. themes.description: I’m not seeing any specific, stock-related news or company announcement for SLDE this week, so the move appears driven mainly by broader risk-on sentiment and short-term trading rather than a clear fundamental catalyst. Overall, the sector is trading more on AI/datacenter optionality and power assets than on spot Bitcoin this week.

themes.name: Property & Casualty Insurance Underwriters
themes.description: The main catalyst is a sector-wide bid into P&C insurers on renewed expectations that “safe” underwriting-driven names will benefit from higher-for-longer rates and firm commercial pricing, pushing capital back into the group after recent underperformance. There is also incremental positive sentiment around balance-sheet strength and capital returns (buybacks/dividends) in high-quality franchises like CB, ALL, ACGL and peers, which are being used as defensive exposure amid macro and election uncertainty. No company-specific headline this week appears to be driving the move; it is a rotation into the group rather than name-specific news.

themes.name: European Bank Stocks
themes.description: The move in European and UK banks this week is being driven primarily by a sharp repricing higher in Eurozone and UK rate-cut expectations being pushed back, steepening yield curves and improving net interest margin outlooks. Earnings season expectations for solid capital returns (buybacks/dividends) at the large European banks are a secondary tailwind.

themes.name: Private Mortgage Insurance
